concept_name: Acceleration as a Function of Velocity — Drag, Damping, Terminal Speed
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.3 — Velocity-dependent acceleration; split from non_uniform_acceleration bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A skydiver jumping from a plane over a Bengaluru airstrip. Gravity pulls her down with 9.8 m/s^2. The faster she falls, the more the air pushes back against her — the drag force grows with speed. Her net acceleration is not constant — it depends on how fast she is currently moving. When her weight exactly balances the drag, her net acceleration drops to zero and she falls at a constant terminal speed — roughly 60 m/s for a human in skydive position.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A car on the Mumbai Expressway with the engine off, coasting. Friction and air drag both oppose motion — and the faster the car rolls, the more drag it feels. So the deceleration is larger at high speed and smaller at low speed. The car does not slow linearly; it slows faster at the start and drifts gently at the end.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A boat on the Ganga cutting the engine. Water drag grows with velocity — the boat decelerates quickly at first, then gradually, asymptotically approaching rest. The acceleration is a function of v, and solving the resulting differential equation gives an exponential decay in speed.

formulas.skydiver_eq: a  =  g  -  (b / m) v     (drag proportional to v)
formulas.solution_of_diff_eq: v(t)  =  v_terminal  +  (v0 - v_terminal) exp(-b t / m)
formulas.separation_of_variables: dv / dt  =  a(v)   =>   dv / a(v)  =  dt   (separate and integrate)

constraints: Technique is 'separation of variables' — move all v terms to one side, all t terms to the other, then integrate
Terminal velocity occurs when acceleration goes to zero; set a(v) = 0 and solve
Approach to terminal velocity is usually exponential, never quite reaching it
For finding v at a specific x rather than t, use v dv/dx = a(v) instead — a hybrid of tricks

STATE_1.title: The Skydiver — Drag Grows with Speed

scene_composition.text:
[PLANE at 4000 m]
        |
        |
        v   (skydiver jumps)
        |
       / \   (falling, body spread)
      \_/
        |
        |                  g = 9.8 m/s^2 down
        v
      GROUND

Forces on her
  Gravity  (down): m g = 70 * 9.8 = 686 N
  Drag     (up):   F_drag = b v

scene_composition.label: F_drag = b v (up, GROWS with v)

scene_composition.text:
Net acceleration
  a  =  g  -  (b/m) v

When v = 0 (just after jump)
  a = g = 9.8   (maximum)

As v grows, drag grows,
so a shrinks.

Eventually drag equals gravity
  (b/m) v = g
  v_terminal = m g / b

At v_terminal,
  a = 0
  skydiver falls at constant speed
  (terminal velocity)

tts_sentences.text_en: A skydiver jumps from a plane at four thousand metres. Two forces act on her while she falls.
Gravity pulls her down — mass times g, about six hundred eighty-six newtons for a seventy-kilogram person. That force is constant.

Air drag pushes her UP, opposing the fall. And critically, drag grows with speed. The faster she falls, the more air she shoves against, and the more drag she feels. A simple model says drag equals b times v.
Net acceleration is gravity minus drag over mass times v — so a depends on v. At the moment of jump, v is zero, drag is zero, and a is the full g.

As she picks up speed, drag builds, and a shrinks. Eventually drag exactly balances gravity, net acceleration is zero, and she stops accelerating. From that point, she falls at a constant terminal velocity.

For a human body in skydive position with drag coefficient about ten, terminal velocity is around sixty metres per second, or two hundred thirty kilometres per hour. That is what seasoned skydivers actually reach.

scene_composition.text:
Terminal velocity

Condition  a = 0
  g - (b/m) v_terminal = 0
  v_terminal = m g / b

For our skydiver (m = 70, g = 9.8, b = 10)
  v_terminal = 70 * 9.8 / 10
             = 68.6 m/s

(Sanity check: real-world skydiver
terminal speed in spread position
is about 55-65 m/s, about 200-230 km/h.
Model matches reality.)

v(t) behaviour

v
|
|   68.6 --(terminal velocity)--
|   /
|  /
| /
|/_______________________  t
0

- Initially (v=0, t=0) slope is g=9.8.
- As t grows, slope reduces.
- Asymptotically approaches 68.6 m/s.
- Never quite reaches it mathematically,
  but gets within 1% in about 5m/b = 35 s.

tts_sentences.text_en: To find terminal velocity, set acceleration to zero and solve for v. This works for ANY a-of-v problem.
g minus b over m times v-terminal equals zero. Solve. v-terminal equals m g over b.
For the skydiver, plug in. Seventy times nine point eight divided by ten equals sixty-eight point six metres per second.

That matches real-world data. Professional skydivers in spread position record terminal speeds of about two hundred to two hundred thirty kilometres per hour. Our simple linear-drag model is in the right range.

On a v-versus-t graph, the curve starts with slope g at the origin, smoothly bends, and flattens toward the horizontal line v equals terminal. The skydiver never quite reaches it — she gets within one per cent in about thirty-five seconds, which is usually enough free-fall time to effectively 'cruise'.

STATE_3.title: The Full Solution — Separation of Variables

scene_composition.text:
Separation of variables technique

Start
  dv/dt  =  a(v)  =  g  -  (b/m) v

Separate (all v on left, all t on right)
  dv / (g - (b/m) v)  =  dt

Integrate both sides
  -(m/b) ln |g - (b/m) v|  =  t  +  C

Apply initial condition v(0) = 0
  -(m/b) ln(g) = C

Solve for v(t)
  v(t)  =  (m g / b)  *  (1  -  exp(-b t / m))
        =  v_terminal  *  (1  -  exp(-t / tau))

where tau = m/b = time constant

At t = 0, v = 0. At t = infinity, v = v_terminal.
For our skydiver, tau = 7 s.
At t = 7 s, v ~ 0.63 * 68.6 = 43 m/s.
At t = 35 s (5 tau), v ~ 99% of terminal.
The Full Solution — Separation of Variables
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: To get v as a function of t, use separation of variables. Move all v-dependent terms to one side and all t-dependent terms to the other.
d v divided by the quantity g minus b over m times v, equals d t.
Integrate both sides. The left side needs a logarithm. The right side is just t plus a constant.
Apply the initial condition — at t equals zero, v equals zero. That fixes the integration constant.

Solve for v of t. You get v-terminal times the quantity one minus e to the minus b t over m. Defining tau as m over b — the time constant — the formula reads v equals v-terminal times one minus e to the minus t over tau.
For the skydiver, tau is seven seconds. At one time constant, v has reached about sixty-three percent of terminal. At five time constants, thirty-five seconds, v is within one percent of terminal.

This exponential-approach behaviour is the signature of a linear drag problem. Water-drag on a boat, current in an R-L circuit, heat cooling a room — all obey the same mathematical form. Recognise it once and it becomes free everywhere.

epic_c_branches.misconception: At terminal velocity, gravity stops pulling the object down
trigger_phrases: why doesn't gravity accelerate at terminal

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — At Terminal Speed, Gravity Must Have Stopped

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

At terminal velocity, the skydiver
no longer accelerates.

If she is not accelerating, gravity
must not be pulling her anymore?
Or maybe she feels weightless?

(No. Gravity is still pulling with
the same m g force. Drag balances
it exactly. Net force is zero, but
individual forces are NOT zero.)
Your Belief — At Terminal Speed, Gravity Must Have Stopped
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A common misconception — at terminal velocity, since the skydiver is no longer accelerating, gravity must no longer be acting on her.

This is false. Gravity is still pulling her down with the full m g force. The reason her acceleration is zero is that drag has GROWN to match gravity. Net force is zero. Individual forces are NOT zero.

STATE_2.title: Force Balance at Terminal — Both Forces Are Large

scene_composition.text:
At terminal velocity

  Net force = m g - b v_t
            = 686 - 686
            = 0

  Net acceleration  =  0 / m  =  0

Gravity did NOT disappear.
Both forces are large.
They cancel exactly.

Skydiver still 'feels' her full weight
of 686 N pressing against the air
under her.

tts_sentences.text_en: At terminal velocity, both forces are large — six hundred eighty-six newtons each, for our skydiver. Gravity pulls down. Drag pushes up.
Sum is zero. Net force is zero. Net acceleration is zero. But individual forces are at full strength.
Gravity did not disappear. It is still there, and it is still m g. The skydiver still 'feels' her weight pressing against the air column below her. She is NOT weightless.
This is a general principle — zero net force does not mean zero individual forces. It means opposing forces have balanced.

scene_composition.text:
Newton's first law

An object moves at constant
velocity if and only if the
NET force on it is zero.

Terminal velocity -> constant velocity.
Constant velocity -> net force zero.
Net force zero -> m g = b v_t
  (individual forces still non-zero,
   just balanced).

This is the same physics as a
book on a table - weight 50 N down,
normal 50 N up, both non-zero,
book stationary.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Newton's first law says an object moves at constant velocity if and only if the NET force on it is zero.
At terminal velocity, the skydiver has constant velocity. So the NET force is zero. The individual forces are still large — m g downward, drag upward, each six hundred eighty-six newtons.

This is the same physics as a book on a table. The book is stationary. Net force is zero. But gravity still pulls it down with fifty newtons, and the table still pushes up with fifty newtons. Both forces are there; they just cancel.
So terminal velocity is not 'gravity turned off'. It is 'drag caught up'.

regeneration_variants.label: Coasting car
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Engine off, decelerating under drag: a = -k v. v(t) = v0 exp(-k t / m). Never reaches zero exactly but becomes practically stopped.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Pure damping: exponential decay toward zero

regeneration_variants.label: Quadratic drag (advanced)
regeneration_variants.physical_world: High-speed case where drag is proportional to v^2. a = g - (c/m) v^2. Solve by separation of variables with tanh solution. Terminal speed = sqrt(m g / c).
